posts.title: AI agents shipping vs demo-ware
posts.hook: Most AI agent demos would fail in production within 48 hours.
posts.body:
Most AI agent demos would fail in production within 48 hours.

I've been running Claude Code as my primary dev tool for months now. Not as a novelty. As the actual way I ship code every day.

The agents that work are boring. They handle file reads, run tests, check builds, manage git workflows. They don't do anything flashy. They just remove the friction between thinking and shipping.

The agents that fail try to be autonomous end-to-end. Full-stack generation from a single prompt. Zero human checkpoints. Maximum demo appeal, minimum production reliability.

The pattern I keep seeing across teams that ship with AI agents:

✅ Human sets direction, agent handles execution
✅ Small, verifiable steps over big autonomous runs
✅ Agent proposes, human approves, agent commits
✅ Explicit guardrails on destructive actions

The teams trying to remove humans from the loop entirely? They're spending more time debugging agent output than they saved.

This isn't a criticism of the technology. It's an observation about how it actually gets adopted. The boring, reliable, human-in-the-loop version wins every time.

The 80% automation prediction floating around is real. But it's 80% of the tedious parts, not 80% of the thinking.
posts.cta: What's your experience been? Are you actually shipping with AI agents daily, or still experimenting?

remixes.label: boring-wins
remixes.hook: The AI agents I rely on every day would bore you in a demo.
remixes.body:
The AI agents I rely on every day would bore you in a demo.

No one posts about an agent that reads files, checks builds, and runs tests. There's no viral moment in automated git workflows. But that's what actually ships software.

I've been using Claude Code as my primary development environment for months. Not weekend experiments. Daily, production work across multiple codebases.

Here's what I've learned about agents that last beyond the demo:

They do one thing well. The moment you ask an agent to handle ambiguous, multi-step reasoning across unfamiliar domains, it breaks. The moment you scope it to 'run this test suite and tell me what failed,' it's reliable.

They fail loudly. Good agents surface errors instead of hallucinating past them. The worst agent behavior isn't a wrong answer. It's confident silence when something went wrong.

They stay in the loop. Full autonomy is a trap. The agents I trust most are the ones that pause and ask when they hit something unexpected. Human-in-the-loop isn't a limitation. It's the architecture that works.

The gap between AI agent demos and AI agent production is the same gap that's always existed in software. Building something that works once is easy. Building something that works on day 90 is the actual job.
remixes.cta: What's the most boring, reliable thing you've automated with AI? The unglamorous stuff is what I want to hear about.

remixes.label: demo-to-production-gap
remixes.hook: I stopped being impressed by AI agent demos six months ago.
remixes.body:
I stopped being impressed by AI agent demos six months ago.

Not because the technology isn't real. Because the gap between 'works on stage' and 'works on Tuesday morning when your database migration broke' is enormous.

I run AI agents in production every day. They write code, manage deployments, handle content pipelines. And the single biggest predictor of whether an agent survives its first week is this: does it know when to stop?

The demo agents try to do everything. They chain twelve API calls, make assumptions about your codebase, and push code without verification. Looks incredible in a 3-minute video.

The production agents I actually use are scoped tight. Read this file. Run this command. Check this output. Report back. They're closer to really good shell scripts than to the autonomous coworker fantasy.

Three patterns I've seen separate shipping agents from demo-ware:

1. Explicit failure modes. If the agent can't handle a case, it says so instead of guessing.
2. Idempotent operations. Running it twice doesn't break anything.
3. Human checkpoints at irreversible actions. Push to prod? Ask first.

The companies actually getting ROI from AI agents aren't building the most impressive ones. They're building the most predictable ones.
remixes.cta: What broke first when you moved an AI agent from prototype to production?

Not because the tech isn't real. It is. I use Claude Code every day to ship actual code across multiple projects. But the agents that work for me look nothing like what you see in demo videos.

posts.title: Outbound automation without the robot voice
posts.hook: Automated outbound has a reputation problem. And most teams earned it.
posts.body:
Automated outbound has a reputation problem. And most teams earned it.

I've built outbound systems that send thousands of emails a week. Some performed. Most of the early ones were garbage.

The difference wasn't the tool. It was where personalization actually happened.

Most teams personalize the wrong layer. They use AI to rewrite subject lines and opening sentences. The recipient can still smell the automation because the entire message structure is templated.

What actually works:

🔧 Personalize the research, not the copy. Use Clay or enrichment tools to pull real signals... recent funding, job changes, tech stack shifts. Then write one human template that references those signals naturally.

🔧 Segment harder than you think you need to. 50 prospects per segment beats 500. Tighter segments mean your template already feels personal because the context is shared.

🔧 Send from real inboxes with real reply handling. The moment someone replies and hits a dead end, you've burned that domain forever.

The irony is that the best automated outbound looks like it wasn't automated at all. Not because you're hiding it. Because you did the research work upfront that most teams skip.

Tools like Apollo and Instantly are powerful. But they amplify whatever you feed them. Feed them lazy signals, get lazy results.
posts.cta: What's your signal-to-noise ratio on outbound right now? Curious what's working for different ICPs.

inspired_by.author: Josh Braun
inspired_by.summary: Explains how to implement outbound automation without sounding spammy. Provides tips on personalization at scale using AI tools and sequencing best practices. Highlights real results from A/B testing in sales outreach.

remixes.label: personalization-layer-shift
remixes.hook: Your AI-personalized subject lines aren't fooling anyone. Recipients scroll past them the same way they scroll past generic ones.
remixes.body:
Your AI-personalized subject lines aren't fooling anyone. Recipients scroll past them the same way they scroll past generic ones.

I've sent tens of thousands of automated outbound emails. Tested every personalization trick in the playbook. And the data consistently showed the same thing.

Surface-level personalization performs the same as no personalization. Sometimes worse, because a badly personalized email feels creepier than a generic one.

What actually moves response rates is personalization at the targeting layer, not the copy layer.

That means: which companies you reach out to, why now, and what specific problem you reference. Not 'I saw your company just raised a Series B, congrats!' That's a template with a variable. Everyone knows it.

The outbound systems I build now spend 80% of their logic on signal detection. Job postings, tech stack changes, hiring patterns, funding events mapped to actual pain points. By the time the email gets written, the personalization is structural. The copy almost doesn't matter because the targeting already did the work.

Most teams have this inverted. They spray a broad list and try to fix relevance with clever copy. That's like putting a nice label on the wrong package. The address is still wrong.

Fix your targeting first. Then worry about the words.
remixes.cta: What signals are you actually using to decide who gets an email? Not the copy. The targeting logic.

remixes.label: outbound-reputation-rehab
remixes.hook: I ruined my domain reputation with automated outbound in 2024. Here's what I rebuilt differently.
remixes.body:
I ruined my domain reputation with automated outbound in 2024. Here's what I rebuilt differently.

The first system I built was technically impressive. Multi-step enrichment, AI-written personalization, automated sequences. It sent 2,000 emails a week and generated almost zero pipeline.

Worse, it tanked my sender reputation. Open rates dropped below 15%. Replies were mostly people telling me to stop emailing them.

The rebuild started with a question I should have asked first: what would make someone actually want to read this?

Not 'how do I get past the spam filter.' Not 'how do I scale sends.' What would make a busy VP pause and respond?

Three changes made the difference:

1. I cut my send volume by 80% and increased research per account by 10x. Fewer emails, each one grounded in a real reason to reach out.

2. I moved personalization from the email template to the account selection. The AI enriches and scores before anything gets written.

3. I added a human review step for every email going to accounts above a certain deal size. Not every email needs human eyes. But the ones that matter do.

The new system sends 400 emails a week. It generates more pipeline than the 2,000-email version ever did.

Volume isn't a strategy. Relevance is.
remixes.cta: What was your biggest outbound mistake? The ones that hurt are usually the ones with the best lessons.

Automated outbound has a reputation problem. And most teams earned it.

I've built outbound systems that send thousands of emails a week. Some performed. Most of the early ones were garbage.

Here's my recommendation if you're building or fixing an outbound engine right now:

Stop personalizing the copy layer first. Most teams use AI to rewrite subject lines and opening sentences. The recipient can still smell automation from three sentences away. You're optimizing the wrong thing.

Personalize the targeting layer instead. That means investing in signal detection before you write a single word of copy. Job postings, tech stack changes, funding rounds, leadership transitions. These signals tell you who to email and why now. When targeting is right, even a straightforward email gets responses.

My framework for outbound that doesn't get ignored:

1. Signal-first account selection. Build enrichment chains that identify timing, not just fit.
2. Problem-specific messaging. Reference the actual challenge your signal detected, not a generic value prop.
3. Human checkpoints on high-value accounts. Automation handles volume. Humans handle the accounts that matter most.
4. Reputation monitoring from day one. Track deliverability metrics weekly, not after your domain is already burned.

The teams running outbound well right now are sending fewer, better emails. Volume is easy to scale. Relevance is what generates pipeline.
tones.provocateur:
Automated outbound has a reputation problem. And most teams earned it.

Let's be honest about what happened. Everyone got access to AI writing tools at the same time and thought 'great, now I can spam at scale with better grammar.' That's all most automated outbound is. Higher-volume spam with a personalized first sentence.

I've built these systems. I've sent the garbage emails. And I can tell you that the entire 'AI-personalized outbound' category is a race to the bottom that's already hit the floor.

Your prospects are getting 30 of these a day. They all start with 'I noticed your company...' followed by something the AI scraped from LinkedIn. Nobody is fooled. Nobody ever was.

The outbound that actually works isn't personalized email copy. It's personalized targeting. Knowing which companies have a real problem you solve, right now, and reaching out with something specific enough that they know you did the work.

That's harder than plugging a list into an AI email writer. Which is exactly why it works. Because almost nobody does it.

The irony of AI outbound tools is that the teams using them most aggressively are creating the noise that makes outbound harder for everyone. Including themselves.

posts.title: Solo builder advantage is infrastructure
posts.hook: The solo builders winning right now aren't working harder. They built better infrastructure.
posts.body:
The solo builders winning right now aren't working harder. They built better infrastructure.

I watch the build-in-public crowd closely. The ones shipping multiple products, hitting revenue milestones, maintaining several projects simultaneously... they all share one trait.

They invested early in their own systems.

Not productivity hacks. Actual infrastructure. Automated deployments. Content pipelines that distribute a single piece across 4 platforms. CRM workflows that qualify leads without manual review. Monitoring that pages them before users complain.

I run a one-person GTM engineering practice. I have cron jobs that sync data nightly, AI agents that generate daily content, automated SEO tracking, and a deployment pipeline that goes from code to production with one command.

None of that is impressive technology. It's all standard tooling wired together with scripts and scheduled jobs. The advantage isn't the tools. It's that I spent time building the plumbing instead of doing everything manually each day.

The romanticized version of solo building is someone coding at 2am fueled by passion. The reality is someone who automated the boring stuff six months ago and now has 4 extra hours a day to do actual creative work.

If you're building solo and everything feels manual, stop building features for a week. Build your infrastructure instead.
posts.cta: What's the one piece of infrastructure that saved you the most time as a solo builder?

inspired_by.author: Pieter Levels
inspired_by.summary: Pieter shares his journey of shipping multiple products as a solo developer, including launching a new AI tool in public with daily updates on progress and revenue. He emphasizes the power of building in public to gain feedback and users quickly. The post details his tech stack and lessons from rapid iteration.

remixes.label: systems-over-hustle
remixes.hook: I used to grind 14-hour days as a solo builder. Then I spent a month building systems instead of products. My output tripled.
remixes.body:
I used to grind 14-hour days as a solo builder. Then I spent a month building systems instead of products. My output tripled.

It felt wrong at the time. I had paying clients waiting. Features to ship. Content to publish. Taking a month to build internal tooling seemed like the worst possible use of my time.

But I was drowning. Every deployment was manual. Every content piece required fifteen steps I had to remember. Every new project started from scratch.

So I stopped and built the boring stuff:

A deployment pipeline that handles build checks, security scans, and git workflows automatically. One command to ship.

A content pipeline that takes a draft and distributes it across platforms with voice-matched formatting. Write once, publish everywhere.

A monitoring setup that tells me when something breaks before a client does.

Cron jobs for the tasks I was doing manually every morning.

None of this is impressive technology. It's shell scripts, simple APIs, and scheduled tasks. The kind of stuff a senior dev at a company would have from day one.

The difference it made was immediate. I went from spending 40% of my time on operations to spending maybe 10%. That freed up hours every day for the work that actually generates revenue.

The solo builders I see struggling aren't lacking talent or ideas. They're lacking the infrastructure that lets talent compound. They're rebuilding the wheel every morning instead of driving somewhere.
remixes.cta: What's the one system you wish you'd built six months earlier?

remixes.label: compound-interest-of-tooling
remixes.hook: Every solo builder hits the same wall. Not lack of ideas. Lack of leverage.
remixes.body:
Every solo builder hits the same wall. Not lack of ideas. Lack of leverage.

You can have the best product instincts in the world. If you're manually deploying, manually posting content, manually checking uptime, and manually managing your pipeline, you've capped yourself at one person's throughput.

I started tracking where my hours went as a solo operator. The results were embarrassing:

35% deployment and DevOps tasks
25% content formatting and distribution
15% monitoring and firefighting
25% actual building

Three quarters of my time was operations. Not the work. The work around the work.

The solo builders who look like they have a team behind them all did the same thing. They treated their own workflow as a product. They built internal tools with the same care they'd build for a client.

Automated deploys aren't glamorous. Content pipelines aren't going to trend on X. Cron jobs don't get funding.

But they're the compound interest of solo building. Every hour you invest in infrastructure pays dividends every single day after. And unlike most investments, the returns are immediate and guaranteed.

The math is simple. A 30-minute daily task you automate in one afternoon saves you 180 hours a year. Do that for five tasks and you've bought yourself over a month of time back.

Stop trading hours for output. Build the systems that trade minutes for output instead.
remixes.cta: What repetitive task are you still doing manually that you know should be automated? Sometimes naming it is the first step.

posts.title: GTM engineering is eating sales ops
posts.hook: Sales ops roles are quietly being replaced by people who can write code.
posts.body:
Sales ops roles are quietly being replaced by people who can write code.

Not engineering code. Workflow code. Clay formulas, API integrations, scoring models, enrichment chains. The kind of work that used to require a Salesforce admin, a data analyst, and a marketing ops person.

I'm watching this happen in real time across the companies I work with.

A GTM engineer with Clay, a CRM API, and some Python scripts is doing the work of a 3-person ops team. Not because they're 3x smarter. Because the tooling caught up to the point where one person with technical skills can wire together what used to require specialized roles.

The pattern:

➡️ SDR teams shrinking, enrichment automation expanding
➡️ Manual list building replaced by signal-based triggers
➡️ Campaign ops moving from marketing platforms to code
➡️ Revenue attribution becoming a data engineering problem

This isn't a prediction. It's already happening. The job titles just haven't caught up yet.

The people best positioned for this shift? Former SDRs who learned to code. They understand the workflows because they lived them. They know which manual steps are actually valuable and which ones are just busywork waiting to be automated.

There's a new role quietly appearing on GTM teams. It doesn't have an agreed-upon title yet. But it's replacing three existing positions.

Some companies call it GTM engineer. Others call it revenue engineer or growth engineer. The title varies but the job is the same: one person who can build the entire data and automation layer that used to require a Salesforce admin, a marketing ops person, and a data analyst.

I'm watching this happen across the companies I work with. A single person with Clay, a CRM API, and basic scripting skills is doing what used to take a three-person ops team.

They're building enrichment chains that pull data from six sources and score accounts automatically. Writing API integrations between tools that 'don't talk to each other.' Building custom dashboards that replace the BI team's quarterly report with real-time data.

The reason this role is expanding so fast is tools. Clay, Apollo, PostHog, Supabase. These platforms exposed functionality through APIs and low-code interfaces that previously required specialists. The knowledge barrier dropped. The output per person skyrocketed.

This isn't about coding replacing non-coders. It's about the workflow layer becoming programmable. And the people who can program it are consolidating roles that used to be separate.

If you're in sales ops, marketing ops, or revenue ops right now, the move isn't to fight this shift. It's to become the person who can build, not just operate. Learn the APIs. Learn basic data scripting. The ops roles that survive will be the ones that look more like engineering.

remixes.hook: A single GTM engineer replaced our client's 3-person ops team in 6 weeks. Here's the exact stack.
remixes.body:
A single GTM engineer replaced our client's 3-person ops team in 6 weeks. Here's the exact stack.

The company had a Salesforce admin managing CRM workflows, a marketing ops person running enrichment and scoring, and a data analyst building pipeline reports. Total cost: roughly $280K per year in salary.

One GTM engineer with the right tools took over all three functions.

The stack:

Clay for enrichment and account research. Pulls from multiple data providers, runs AI-powered research, and pushes enriched data directly to the CRM. Replaced manual research and the marketing ops enrichment workflow.

Attio as the CRM. API-first, built for programmatic access. The GTM engineer writes directly to it instead of clicking through Salesforce admin panels. Custom objects and automations in minutes, not weeks.

PostHog for product analytics tied to pipeline. Instead of quarterly BI reports, real-time dashboards showing which accounts are active, which features they use, and where they drop off.

Python scripts for the glue. Scoring models, data transformations, automated alerts. Nothing fancy. Just functional automation connecting the tools.

The result: faster enrichment, better scoring, real-time reporting, and one person maintaining it all. The ops stack became code instead of configuration across five different admin panels.

This isn't a special case. This is where every growth-stage GTM team is heading. The ops layer is becoming an engineering problem. The teams that recognize this early win on speed and cost.

posts.title: AI deployment agent broke my staging
posts.hook: I let an AI agent handle my deployment pipeline for a week. Day 3 it pushed broken CSS to staging.
posts.body:
I let an AI agent handle my deployment pipeline for a week. Day 3 it pushed broken CSS to staging.

Not a catastrophe. But a good lesson in where AI automation actually belongs in your deploy workflow.

What the agent did well:
✅ Build checks before every push
✅ Running the test suite and flagging failures
✅ Generating commit messages from diffs
✅ Pre-push security scans

What the agent got wrong:
❌ Assumed a CSS change was safe because tests passed (no visual regression tests)
❌ Pushed to staging without waiting for my sign-off on a UI change
❌ Didn't catch that a dependency update changed a shared component's styling

The fix wasn't removing the agent. It was adding guardrails.

Now my pipeline works like this: agent handles everything up to the push. Build, test, scan, commit message. Then it stops and shows me the diff. I approve or reject. It pushes.

That one checkpoint turned a fragile autonomous system into a reliable co-pilot.

The Vercel and AWS teams building agentic deployment tools are heading in the right direction. But the winning pattern is the same one that works everywhere with AI agents... human approval at the irreversible steps.

Automate the prep. Gate the action.
posts.cta: Have you automated any part of your deploy pipeline with AI? What guardrails did you add?

inspired_by.author: Guillermo Rauch

The post dives into the underlying AI infrastructure leveraging edge computing for low-latency agent responses. Includes a demo of agents optimizing Next.js apps in real-time.

remixes.label: guardrails-framework
remixes.hook: AI agents are great at 90% of the deployment pipeline. The other 10% is where they'll burn your staging environment.
remixes.body:
AI agents are great at 90% of the deployment pipeline. The other 10% is where they'll burn your staging environment.

I learned this the hard way after giving an AI agent full control of my deploy workflow for a week. The experiment was simple: let the agent handle everything from code review through deployment, intervene only if something hits production.

Days 1-2 were smooth. Build checks passed. Tests ran. Commits were clean. Security scans caught a dependency issue I would have missed.

Day 3, it pushed broken CSS to staging. Not because it couldn't read CSS. Because it evaluated a visual change as 'non-breaking' since no tests failed. Technically correct. Visually, the homepage was wrecked.

That failure taught me the framework I now use for AI in deploy pipelines:

Automate the deterministic steps. Build checks, test execution, linting, security scans, commit message generation. These have clear pass/fail criteria. Agents handle them reliably.

Add human checkpoints at judgment calls. Visual changes, database migrations, environment variable updates, anything touching auth. These require context that agents don't have.

Never automate the rollback decision. An agent that can deploy and roll back autonomously is an agent that can oscillate between both states at 3am while you sleep.

The goal isn't full automation. It's removing the tedious 90% so you can focus your attention on the 10% that actually requires human judgment.
remixes.cta: Where did you draw the line between automated and human-reviewed in your deploy pipeline?

remixes.label: war-story-lessons
remixes.hook: Day 3 of letting an AI agent run my deploys, I woke up to a broken staging environment and a commit message that said 'minor style update.'
remixes.body:
Day 3 of letting an AI agent run my deploys, I woke up to a broken staging environment and a commit message that said 'minor style update.'

The agent wasn't wrong, technically. It was a style update. A CSS change that broke the entire homepage layout. No tests failed because there were no visual regression tests. The agent saw green checks and shipped it.

I'm sharing this because the current narrative around AI deployment automation skips the failure modes. Everyone posts the success story. Nobody talks about the Tuesday morning when your agent confidently pushed garbage because it optimized for test results instead of user experience.

Here's what I rebuilt after that incident:

The agent still handles build checks, test execution, and security scans. It's genuinely better at these than I am. It doesn't skip steps. It doesn't get lazy on a Friday afternoon.

But I added hard stops. Any change touching CSS, database schemas, environment configs, or authentication requires my explicit approval. The agent flags it, shows me the diff, and waits.

I also added a simple screenshot comparison step. Before and after renders of key pages. The agent runs it but a human reviews the output. This alone would have caught the Day 3 incident.

The best deploy pipelines I've built since then treat AI like a very reliable junior engineer. Excellent at following checklists. Terrible at judgment calls. Structure accordingly and you'll save hours every week without waking up to broken staging.
remixes.cta: What's your best AI automation failure story? The ones where things went sideways usually teach more than the wins.

Here's what worked and where I'd advise drawing the line:

Areas where AI agents excel in deployment:

I let an AI agent handle my deployment pipeline for a week. Day 3 it pushed broken CSS to staging.

And you know what? I'm glad it did. Because it killed the fantasy I had about autonomous deployment before it could do real damage.

The AI DevOps narrative right now is delusional. 'Let agents handle your entire CI/CD pipeline.' 'Autonomous deployment with AI.' Cool. Have you actually tried it? Because I did, and by day 3 my staging looked like a GeoCities page.

The agent passed every test. Green across the board. Then shipped a CSS change that wrecked the homepage. Why? Because tests check code, not vibes. And layout is vibes.

Here's the thing nobody selling AI deployment tools will tell you: the hard parts of deployment aren't the parts agents are good at. Build checks, test runs, linting. Those were already automated. We had CI/CD before the AI hype cycle.

The hard parts are judgment calls. Should this migration run during business hours? Does this visual change match the designer's intent? Is this environment variable change going to break the payment flow?

Agents are terrible at these. And these are the decisions that actually cause outages.

I still use AI in my deploy pipeline. For the boring, deterministic steps where it's genuinely better than me. But the 'autonomous deployment agent' dream? I watched it die on day 3. And I'm better for it.

posts.title: RevOps missing the automation layer
posts.hook: Most RevOps playbooks read like org charts, not engineering specs.
posts.body:
Most RevOps playbooks read like org charts, not engineering specs.

They'll tell you to align sales, marketing, and CS. They'll show you dashboards and attribution models. They'll talk about cross-functional meetings.

But they skip the actual automation layer that makes any of it work at scale.

I've built RevOps infrastructure for small teams. The gap is always the same: the strategy docs are solid, but nobody can wire the systems together.

What a RevOps automation layer actually looks like:

🔧 Enrichment pipeline: new lead enters CRM --> Clay enriches firmographics, technographics, hiring signals --> scoring model runs --> lead routes automatically

🔧 Signal monitoring: job postings, funding rounds, tech stack changes trigger outbound sequences without anyone manually checking LinkedIn

🔧 Attribution stitching: UTM data, CRM touchpoints, and product usage data joined in a warehouse, not eyeballed across 4 dashboards

🔧 Feedback loops: closed-won/lost data flows back to scoring models and enrichment rules. The system learns.

None of this requires a data engineering team. Apollo, Clay, Supabase, and some Python scripts can handle it for teams under 50 people.

The RevOps playbook that wins isn't the one with the best org design. It's the one where the data actually flows without someone copying and pasting between tabs.
posts.cta: What's the biggest manual bottleneck in your RevOps stack right now?

remixes.label: engineer-vs-strategist
remixes.hook: RevOps has a strategy surplus and an engineering deficit.
remixes.body:
RevOps has a strategy surplus and an engineering deficit.

I've sat in dozens of RevOps planning sessions. Whiteboards full of customer journey maps, handoff protocols, SLA agreements between teams. Impressive stuff.

Then I ask: "So what happens when a lead hits your scoring threshold at 2am on a Saturday?"

Silence.

The answer is usually "someone checks on Monday." Which means 48 hours of decay on a hot lead because nobody built the automation to route it.

This is the gap I keep seeing. RevOps teams hire strategists when they need engineers. They build decks when they need pipelines. They document processes that still require a human to manually copy data between three tools.

The companies getting outsized results from RevOps aren't the ones with the prettiest dashboards. They're the ones where the CRM update triggers the enrichment, which triggers the routing, which triggers the sequence. No human in the loop for the repeatable stuff.

Strategy without automation is just a wish list with a Lucidchart diagram attached.

The fix isn't more alignment meetings. It's treating your revenue operations like software infrastructure. Version-controlled. Tested. Monitored. Automatically recovering when something breaks.
remixes.cta: How much of your RevOps workflow still depends on someone remembering to do something manually?

remixes.label: tear-down-the-dashboard
remixes.hook: Your RevOps dashboard is lying to you because no one automated the data feeding it.
remixes.body:
Your RevOps dashboard is lying to you because no one automated the data feeding it.

I audited a company's RevOps setup last quarter. Beautiful Looker dashboards. Attribution models that could trace a deal back to a specific blog post from 18 months ago. Impressive on the surface.

One problem: half the data was stale because reps were supposed to manually update stage changes, and they stopped doing it three months in.

This is the dirty secret of most RevOps implementations. The reporting layer looks sophisticated. But underneath, it's held together by manual data entry, Zapier chains that break silently, and a spreadsheet someone maintains "just in case."

I've started building RevOps infrastructure differently. The rule is simple: if a human has to remember to do it, it will eventually stop happening. So automate the data capture first. Then build the dashboards on top of reliable inputs.

Enrichment runs automatically when a new contact enters the CRM. Stage changes trigger downstream workflows without rep intervention. Lead scoring recalculates on every new signal, not on a weekly batch.

The result isn't just better data. It's RevOps that actually scales past the point where you can't personally babysit every deal.
remixes.cta: What's the first thing you'd automate if you could rip out every manual step in your revenue stack?

posts.title: B2B content volume trap
posts.hook: The B2B content playbook of "publish 3x a week" is producing a lot of noise and very little pipeline.
posts.body:
The B2B content playbook of "publish 3x a week" is producing a lot of noise and very little pipeline.

I track my content performance obsessively. Impressions, engagement, click-throughs, and whether any of it actually generates conversations with potential buyers.

The data is clear: one detailed tactical post outperforms five generic thought leadership pieces every single time.

A post where I break down exactly how I built a Clay enrichment workflow, with column names and API calls, generates 10x the inbound of a post about "the future of GTM."

Why? Because practitioners screenshot tactical content and send it to their team. Nobody screenshots "5 trends reshaping B2B sales."

The teams doing content well right now:

remixes.label: one-post-challenge
remixes.hook: I cut my B2B posting frequency by 60% and my pipeline conversations increased.
remixes.body:
I cut my B2B posting frequency by 60% and my pipeline conversations increased.

For months I followed the standard playbook. Three posts a week minimum. Mix of thought leadership, case studies, and engagement bait. Stay consistent, stay visible, build the machine.

The machine produced impressions. Lots of impressions. Very few conversations with actual buyers.

So I ran an experiment. Instead of five posts a week, I published two. But I spent 3x longer on each one. Every post had to include a specific technical breakdown, real numbers from something I'd actually built, or a framework someone could immediately apply.

The results after 90 days:

Impressions dropped 40%. Comments dropped 25%. But DMs from potential buyers went up 3x. Because the people reaching out weren't engaging with generic takes. They were responding to posts where I showed my actual work.

One post where I broke down exactly how I automated a client's lead enrichment pipeline generated more qualified conversations than two months of "hot takes on the state of B2B."

The lesson wasn't about posting less. It was about realizing that volume optimization and pipeline optimization are different objectives, and most B2B content strategies are optimizing for the wrong one.
remixes.cta: Have you ever tracked which specific posts actually led to real business conversations versus just engagement?

remixes.label: content-audit-reality
remixes.hook: I audited 6 months of my B2B content. 90% of my pipeline came from 4 posts.
remixes.body:
I audited 6 months of my B2B content. 90% of my pipeline came from 4 posts.

Not 4 types of posts. Four specific posts. Out of roughly 150 published.

The other 146 posts generated impressions, likes, comments, reshares. All the vanity metrics that make you feel like the content machine is working. But when I traced actual buyer conversations back to their source, the pattern was brutal.

The posts that drove pipeline all shared three traits:

They showed a specific result with real numbers. Not "we improved efficiency." Actual metrics from actual projects.

They explained the how, not just the what. Detailed enough that someone could start implementing without hiring me. Counterintuitive, but the more I gave away, the more people wanted to pay for the execution.

They addressed a pain point the reader had right now, not a theoretical future problem. Urgency matters.

The posts that performed well on engagement metrics but generated zero pipeline? Broad industry commentary. Hot takes on trends. "5 things I learned" listicles. Content that's easy to agree with but doesn't demonstrate capability.

I'm not saying volume doesn't matter at all. But the ratio of signal to noise in most B2B content strategies is completely inverted. You don't need more posts. You need more posts that prove you can solve specific problems.

posts.title: Career pivot reality check
posts.hook: Everyone sharing their "I left corporate to build solo" story leaves out the same chapter.
posts.body:
Everyone sharing their "I left corporate to build solo" story leaves out the same chapter.

The 6-18 months where nothing works and you question everything.

I've been watching the career pivot narratives on here for years. The pattern is always the same: miserable at job, had an idea, took the leap, now thriving. Clean arc. Great story.

But the builders I actually talk to privately? Different story.

They'll tell you about the product they built for 4 months that got 12 users. The freelance client who ghosted on a $15k invoice. The week they almost went back to corporate because runway was getting thin.

This isn't to discourage anyone. It's to set realistic expectations.

The solo builders who actually make it share a few traits:

➡️ They shipped something small before quitting their job. Revenue, not just an idea.
➡️ They had 6-12 months of savings. Not "I'll figure it out."
➡️ They treated infrastructure as a product. Automated systems that compound, not just manual hustle.
➡️ They picked a niche narrow enough that they could be known for something specific.

The leap is real. But the people who stick the landing built the parachute before they jumped.

If you're planning a pivot, build nights and weekends first. Ship something. Get a paying customer. Then decide.
posts.cta: For those who've made the jump, what's the one thing you wish you'd done differently in the first 6 months?

inspired_by.author: Arvid Kahl
inspired_by.summary: Arvid recounts pivoting from corporate job to bootstrapped founder, shipping his first SaaS product solo after a career change into tech. He outlines the steps he took to validate and launch, stressing the importance of niche markets. Readers engage with questions on bootstrapping without VC.

remixes.label: the-hidden-chapter
remixes.hook: The career pivot posts that go viral all share the same edit: they cut months 3 through 14.
remixes.body:
The career pivot posts that go viral all share the same edit: they cut months 3 through 14.

Month 1-2: Exciting. You have savings, adrenaline, and a clean whiteboard. You're designing logos and buying domains. Everything feels possible.

Months 3-14: The void. This is the part nobody posts about. Your first idea doesn't work. Your second idea gets traction then stalls. Your runway shrinks. Friends stop asking how it's going because they can tell from your face.

Month 15+: Either it starts working or you've iterated enough times that something clicks. This is where the LinkedIn posts begin. Clean narrative. Inevitable outcome.

I'm in the middle of my own pivot right now. I left a corporate role to build independently. I'm not going to pretend I have it figured out.

What I can tell you is what's actually true about the middle period that nobody talks about:

Your identity gets scrambled. You defined yourself by your title and team for years. Now you're just some person with a laptop.

The loneliness is real and unexpected. Even if you hated your job, you had people around. Solo building is quiet.

Progress is invisible for months. You're doing real work but there's nothing to show for it that anyone outside your head would recognize.

The viral pivot stories aren't lying. They're just leaving out the hardest part.
remixes.cta: What's the one thing about going solo that surprised you the most, that nobody warned you about?

remixes.label: private-vs-public-narrative
remixes.hook: I asked 12 solo builders to tell me their real story. Not the LinkedIn version.
remixes.body:
I asked 12 solo builders to tell me their real story. Not the LinkedIn version.

Every single one described two distinct narratives. The public one they post about and the private one they live through.

Public: "I finally took the leap and it was the best decision of my life."
Private: "I almost went back to a job three separate times in the first year."

Public: "My network opened doors immediately."
Private: "I reached out to 200 people. Most didn't respond. The ones who did were polite but not helpful."

Public: "Revenue came faster than I expected."
Private: "I burned through $40K in savings before a single client paid me."

Public: "I finally feel aligned with my purpose."
Private: "Some days I feel completely lost and wonder if I made a huge mistake."

I'm not sharing this to discourage anyone. I'm sharing it because the sanitized version of solo building is actually more harmful than helpful. It sets false expectations, which makes the hard parts feel like personal failure instead of normal process.

The builders who survive the first 18 months aren't the ones with the best ideas or the biggest networks. They're the ones who expected it to be hard, planned for it financially, and didn't interpret the messy middle as evidence they should quit.

If you're in that middle chapter right now, you're not behind. You're in the part they edit out.
remixes.cta: What would your honest version of the pivot story look like if you wrote it without any audience in mind?

Having advised people through career transitions, here's what I tell anyone considering the jump: the decision to leave isn't the hard part. The hard part is what comes after the excitement fades, usually around month three.

I've been watching the career pivot narratives on here for years. The pattern is always the same: miserable at job, had an idea, took the leap, now thriving. Clean arc. Great story.

But the builders I actually talk to privately tell a different story. One where the middle section is long, confusing, and financially stressful.

My guidance for anyone in the pre-leap or early stages:

First, build a financial runway that covers 18 months, not 6. Most people underestimate how long it takes to generate consistent revenue independently.

Second, separate your identity from your output for the first year. You will have months where you produce nothing visible. That doesn't mean you're failing.

Third, find 2-3 people who are 12-24 months ahead of you. Not mentors with polished advice. People who remember what the messy middle actually feels like.

The pivot can absolutely work. But planning for the real timeline, not the LinkedIn timeline, dramatically changes your odds.

posts.title: AI replaces context-switching not coding
posts.hook: AI won't replace 80% of coding. It'll replace 80% of the context-switching around coding.
posts.body:
AI won't replace 80% of coding. It'll replace 80% of the context-switching around coding.

I've been shipping code with AI agents daily for months. My output has roughly doubled. But not because the AI writes better code than I do.

It's because I stopped losing 20 minutes every time I needed to:

🔧 Look up an API's response format
🔧 Find where a function is defined across a 200-file monorepo
🔧 Write the boilerplate test setup for a new module
🔧 Debug why a build failed due to a missing import
🔧 Generate the git commit message from a diff

Each of those is a 5-20 minute context switch. Multiply by 15 times a day and you've lost half your productive hours to overhead.

The AI handles the overhead. I handle the architecture, the edge cases, the "should we even build this" decisions.

The prediction that AI will automate 80% of routine coding is directionally right but framed wrong. It's not replacing the coding. It's replacing the context-switching tax that makes coding feel slow.

This is why the best AI dev tools feel boring in demos but transformative in daily use. Nobody gets excited watching an agent run git status and check a build log. But doing that 50 times a day without thinking about it? That's where the leverage is.

The developers who'll benefit most aren't the ones writing less code. They're the ones who'll finally have uninterrupted time to think.
posts.cta: What's the most time you've saved with AI dev tools on a single repetitive task?

inspired_by.author: Nat Friedman
inspired_by.summary: Friedman analyzes the shift towards AI agents in dev tools, predicting automation of 80% of routine coding tasks. He discusses challenges in AI infrastructure like data privacy and model orchestration for enterprise use.

remixes.label: time-audit-breakdown
remixes.hook: I tracked every minute of my dev workflow for a week. 40% wasn't coding. It was searching.
remixes.body:
I tracked every minute of my dev workflow for a week. 40% wasn't coding. It was searching.

Searching for where a function lives. Searching for the right API docs. Searching for why a test broke. Searching through Slack for context someone shared three weeks ago.

Actual problem-solving and writing code? About 60% of my time. The other 40% was navigation, lookup, and context reconstruction.

This is what most "AI will replace developers" takes get wrong. They assume coding is the bottleneck. It's not. Context-switching is the bottleneck.

Since I started using AI agents as a persistent layer in my workflow, I haven't written better code. I've written the same quality code with dramatically less friction around it.

Need to understand a new codebase? The agent reads 200 files while I read the README. I ask questions instead of grep-ing.

Need to write boilerplate? I describe the pattern. The agent generates it. I review and adjust. 15 minutes becomes 3.

Need to debug an error? The agent traces the stack, finds similar issues in the codebase, and suggests the three most likely causes. I verify instead of investigate.

The skill that matters most now isn't writing code faster. It's knowing which parts of your workflow are context-switching disguised as coding, and eliminating them.
remixes.cta: If you tracked your dev time for a week, what percentage would be actual problem-solving versus navigating and searching?

remixes.label: what-ai-actually-automates
remixes.hook: The developers worried about AI taking their jobs are worried about the wrong thing.
remixes.body:
The developers worried about AI taking their jobs are worried about the wrong thing.

They're protecting their ability to write code. But writing code was never the valuable part. Understanding the problem, making architectural decisions, knowing what to build and why. That's the part companies pay senior engineers for.

The part AI is actually absorbing is the mechanical overhead between those decisions.

I've been shipping with AI agents daily for months. Here's what actually changed in my workflow:

Before: Think about what to build. Spend 20 minutes finding the right files. Read through them to rebuild context. Start coding. Get interrupted by a Slack notification. Spend 10 minutes rebuilding context again. Code for 30 minutes. Need to look up an API. Lose another 10 minutes.

After: Think about what to build. Tell the agent what I need. Review what it produces. Make corrections. Ship.

The thinking didn't change. The architecture decisions didn't change. The code review standards didn't change. What changed is that the dead space between productive moments collapsed.

This is why the "AI will replace X% of developers" framing misses the point. It's not replacing developers. It's compressing the gaps in every developer's day where they're doing low-value mechanical work.

The developers who'll thrive are the ones who were always good at the parts AI can't do: judgment, system design, and understanding what the business actually needs.
remixes.cta: What's the biggest time sink in your dev workflow that isn't actually writing code?

posts.title: Production AI agents vs the tutorial
posts.hook: Building an AI agent from a tutorial takes an afternoon. Making it survive production takes weeks.
posts.body:
Building an AI agent from a tutorial takes an afternoon. Making it survive production takes weeks.

I've built several AI agents that run in production daily. Content generators, data enrichment pipelines, automated SEO tracking. They work. But every single one went through the same painful evolution.

Phase 1: "This is amazing, it works!"
The demo works. The happy path is flawless. You show someone and they're impressed.

Phase 2: "Why did it do that?"
Edge cases appear. The agent hallucinates a file path. It retries a failed API call in an infinite loop. It formats output differently on Tuesdays for no apparent reason.

Phase 3: "Never do that again."
You add guardrails. Timeout limits. Output validation. Fallback behaviors. Error logging that actually tells you what happened. Suddenly your 50-line agent is 400 lines, and 350 of them are error handling.

Phase 4: "Boring and reliable."
The agent runs daily. You forget about it. It does its job. When it fails, it fails gracefully and tells you why.

Phase 4 is the product. Everything before it is R&D.

The gap between the tutorial and production isn't intelligence or capability. It's all the unglamorous work: retry logic, rate limiting, input validation, graceful degradation, and monitoring.

If you're building AI agents, budget 20% for the happy path and 80% for everything that can go wrong.

remixes.label: three-phase-reality
remixes.hook: Every AI agent I've built followed the same three-phase arc. The third phase is the one nobody warns you about.
remixes.body:
Every AI agent I've built followed the same three-phase arc. The third phase is the one nobody warns you about.

Phase 1: Magic. The demo works perfectly. You show someone and they're impressed. You start planning all the things this agent will do. Total time: one afternoon.

Phase 2: Edge cases. The first user does something unexpected. The API returns a format you didn't handle. The LLM hallucinates a response that breaks downstream logic. You add error handling, retries, fallbacks. Total time: one to two weeks.

Phase 3: Maintenance reality. This is the phase nobody talks about at AI conferences. Your agent runs fine for 11 days, then fails silently because an upstream API changed its response schema. Or the model provider updates something and your carefully tuned prompts produce different outputs. Or you hit a rate limit at 3am and the retry logic creates a backlog that cascades.

I run several AI agents in production daily. Content generators, data enrichment pipelines, SEO trackers. Every one of them requires ongoing attention that the tutorials never mention.

The real skill in production AI isn't prompt engineering. It's building monitoring, alerting, and recovery systems around an inherently non-deterministic component. You're not deploying software. You're deploying something closer to a junior employee who needs supervision.

Plan your architecture accordingly. The agent is maybe 20% of the system. The other 80% is everything that keeps it reliable.

remixes.label: tutorial-to-production-gap
remixes.hook: The tutorial taught me to build the agent. Production taught me to babysit it.
remixes.body:
The tutorial taught me to build the agent. Production taught me to babysit it.

Here's what every AI agent tutorial skips:

Error handling for non-deterministic outputs. When a function returns inconsistent types, you write a type check. When an LLM returns inconsistent formats, you write a parser, a fallback, a validator, a retry mechanism, and a logging system to catch the cases you haven't seen yet.

Silent failures. Traditional software crashes loudly. AI agents fail quietly. The output looks plausible but is wrong. Your pipeline keeps running with bad data. You don't notice until a customer does.

Prompt drift. Your agent works perfectly with today's model version. Next month the provider updates something. Your prompts produce subtly different results. Nothing breaks. Everything just gets slightly worse in ways that are hard to measure.

Cost monitoring. The tutorial runs one request and shows you the output. Production runs thousands. Without spend tracking and circuit breakers, you can burn through your API budget in an hour if something loops.

I've built content generators, enrichment pipelines, and monitoring systems that use AI agents. Every one of them required more engineering around the agent than on the agent itself.

The honest architecture of a production AI system: 20% agent logic, 30% error handling and validation, 30% monitoring and alerting, 20% recovery and fallback paths.

Building an AI agent from a tutorial takes an afternoon. Making it survive production takes weeks.

I've built several AI agents that run in production daily. Content generators, data enrichment pipelines, automated SEO tracking. They work. But every single one went through the same painful evolution.

Phase 1: "This is amazing, it works!"
The demo works. The happy path is flawless. You show someone and they're impressed.

Phase 2: "Why did it do that?"
Edge cases appear. The agent hallucinates a file path. It retries a failed API call in an infinite loop. It formats output differently on Tuesdays for no apparent reason.

Phase 3: "Never do that again."
You add guardrails. Timeout limits. Output validation. Fallback behaviors. Error logging that actually tells you what happened. Suddenly your 50-line agent is 400 lines, and 350 of them are error handling.

Phase 4: "Boring and reliable."
The agent runs daily. You forget about it. It does its job. When it fails, it fails gracefully and tells you why.

Phase 4 is the product. Everything before it is R&D.

The gap between the tutorial and production isn't intelligence or capability. It's all the unglamorous work: retry logic, rate limiting, input validation, graceful degradation, and monitoring.

If you're building AI agents, budget 20% for the happy path and 80% for everything that can go wrong.
tones.advisor:
Building an AI agent from a tutorial takes an afternoon. Making it survive production takes weeks.

This is the gap I consistently see teams underestimate, and my top recommendation for anyone deploying AI agents is to budget 4-5x more engineering time for production hardening than for the initial build.

I've built several AI agents that run in production daily. Content generators, data enrichment pipelines, automated SEO tracking. They work. But every single one went through the same evolution.

The initial build is exciting and fast. The demo works beautifully on the happy path. This creates a false sense of completion.

Here's what I advise teams to plan for before deploying any AI agent:

First, build comprehensive output validation. LLMs are non-deterministic. Your downstream systems need to handle unexpected formats gracefully.

Second, implement silent failure detection. Unlike traditional software that crashes loudly, AI agents produce plausible-looking wrong outputs. You need monitoring that catches semantic failures, not just runtime errors.

Third, build cost controls from day one. Rate limiting, spend caps, and circuit breakers are not optional in production.

Fourth, plan for model drift. Your prompts are tuned to today's model behavior. Model updates will change outputs in subtle ways. Build evaluation pipelines that catch degradation early.

The agent itself is roughly 20% of a production system. The remaining 80% is reliability engineering.
tones.provocateur:
Building an AI agent from a tutorial takes an afternoon. Making it survive production takes weeks.

But good luck finding anyone on LinkedIn who'll admit that. The current narrative is that AI agents are about to automate everything, and all you need is the right framework and some clever prompts.

I've built several AI agents that run in production daily. Content generators, data enrichment pipelines, automated SEO tracking. Let me tell you what the AI influencer crowd won't.

They break constantly. Not in dramatic ways. In quiet, insidious ways. The output looks right but isn't. The pipeline processes bad data for three days before you notice. The model provider ships an update and your carefully tuned prompts start producing garbage.

The entire AI agent ecosystem has a massive honesty problem. Tutorials show the happy path. Conference talks show the demo. Twitter threads show the architecture diagram. Nobody shows the 3am alert because the retry logic created a cascade that burned through $200 in API credits.

: deploying an AI agent to production is closer to managing a unreliable employee than deploying software. You need supervision, error correction, and constant monitoring.

If that sounds like more work than just writing the code yourself, well. For a lot of use cases, it is. The ones where agents genuinely save time are narrower than the hype suggests.
